title: Fixing the LinkedList Duplicate Value Check

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: Input is given in two parts: the first line has the number of nodes (n), the second line has n space-separated integers representing node values.
statement.outputFormat: Output is a boolean value indicating whether the list contains duplicate values.
statement.notes: This problem doesn't require handling edge cases, but you must correctly identify duplicate values

examples.explanation: The list does not contain any duplicate values

hints.text: Review the implementation of the checkDuplicates function to understand the issue

editorial.title: Explanation of Correct Approach
editorial.intuition: To fix the implementation, you need to ensure that you are correctly iterating over the list and checking for duplicates. This can be done by iterating using a node and its next node, not just one node.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute-force approach would involve checking each node with the next node, which is already implemented but has a bug in the logic.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use a Set to keep track of unique values seen so far.
editorial.optimalSolution: An optimal solution would also use a Set but would avoid extra space by maintaining a set of nodes instead of values.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Proof of Correctness can be shown by demonstrating that the function correctly identifies all cases of duplicate values and handles edge cases.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run can help understand the execution of the function and identify any logical errors.
commonMistakes: Not correctly iterating over the list

starterCode.cpp: class Solution {public: bool checkDuplicates(ListNode* head) { ListNode* current = head; while (current != nullptr) { while (current->next != nullptr) { if (current->val == current->next->val) { return true; } current = current->next; } current = head; } return false; }};
starterCode.java: public class Solution {public boolean checkDuplicates(ListNode head) { ListNode current = head; while (current != null) { while (current.next != null) { if (current.val == current.next.val) { return true; } current = current.next; } current = head; } return false; }};
starterCode.python: class Solution: def checkDuplicates(self, head: ListNode) -> bool: current = head while current: while current.next: if current.val == current.next.val: return True current = current.next current = head return False
starterCode.javascript: var checkDuplicates = function(head) { var current = head; while (current) { while (current.next) { if (current.val == current.next.val) { return true; } current = current.next; } current = head; } return false; };

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution {public: bool checkDuplicates(ListNode* head) { ListNode* current = head; while (current != nullptr) { while (current->next != nullptr) { if (current->val == current->val) { return true; } current = current->next; } current = head; } return false; }};
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution {public boolean checkDuplicates(ListNode head) { ListNode current = head; while (current != null) { while (current.next != null) { if (current.val == current.val) { return true; } current = current.next; } current = head; } return false; }};
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def checkDuplicates(self, head: ListNode) -> bool: current = head while current: while current.next: if current.val == current.val: return True current = current.next current = head return False
buggyStarterCode.javascript: var checkDuplicates = function(head) { var current = head; while (current) { while (current.next) { if (current.val == current.val) { return true; } current = current.next; } current = head; } return false; };
